data.title: Warren says U.S. reserve fund shouldn't be used to boost Venezuela investors
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) has urged the U.S. Treasury Department against using the Exchange Stabilization Fund ("ESF") to support financial institutions considering investments in Venezuela, arguing it shouldn't be used to enrich hedge funds, according to a media report on Tuesday.

The news comes after the arrest and capture of Venezuela's president by U.S. forces early this month.

Established in the 1930s, the ESF is a Treasury reserve fund used to help stabilize financial markets and currencies. It was used in 2025 to a secure a multibillion-dollar swap line with Argentina. 

“President Trump should not launch illegal foreign wars or deploy taxpayer resources to facilitate Wall Street war profiteering, especially as his economic policies are failing here at home,” Warren, the ranking member of the Senate Committee on Banking, and Urban Affairs, wrote in the letter to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Bloomberg reported.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: SA analysts see bullish year ahead for S&P 500
data.description:
Seeking Alpha analysts are overwhelmingly bullish that the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) will gain more than 5% this year, according to a recent survey.

Of the 97 analysts polled, 73.2% saw the S&P climbing more than 5% in 2026, while 14.4% expected the index to slide more than 5%, and 11.3% didn't see it rising or falling more than 5%.

The average analyst target for the index was 7,432, with a median estimate of 7,553. SA analysts Andrew Hecht and Geneva Investor had the highest target prediction, with both forecasting 10,000, while Damir Tokic had the lowest at 4,100.

Of the 71 analysts who indicated they were bullish about the index's performance this year, eight said they expected the S&P 500 to soar 20% or more in 2026, while 17 predicted a jump of 15% to 20%. Another 25, or 25.8%, were moderately bullish, forecasting a gain of 10% to 15%, with an additional 22 expecting an increase of 5% to 10%.

As for the bears, two of the 97 analysts polled expected the index to dive 25% or more, while another two saw the index dropping 15% to 20%. Four of the analysts saw the index sliding 10% to 15%, while six expected it to dip 5% to 10%.

data.title: Goldman Sachs expects IBM's upcoming 2026 guidance to reflect improving demand
data.description:
IBM (IBM) is slated to release its fourth quarter 2025 financial results on January 28, and the analysts at Goldman Sachs expect Big Blue's 2026 guidance to demonstrate stronger demand.

"We expect 2026 guidance to show modest organic revenue acceleration on gradually improving customer demand, although enterprises will likely continue to rationalize discretionary IT budgets given the ongoing impact of tariffs, shifting government policies, and AI disruption," said Goldman Sachs analysts, led by James Schneider, in an investor note. "Given the apparent stabilization in fundamentals in recent months, we believe investor expectations have increased in recent weeks - although concerns about AI's negative impact on the sector are still near peak."

Goldman Sachs maintained its Buy rating and $350 price target. However, it slightly increased some of its earnings per share estimates for the years ahead. It raised its full-year 2026 EPS to $11.50 from $11.40, kept 2027 at $12.45 and increased 2028 to $13.30 from $13.20.

"For 2026, we expect IBM to guide for constant currency revenue growth consistent with its mid-single digit range (we model 4.4% CC growth or $70.3 bn vs. the Street at $70.2 bn), along with a positive FX impact of roughly 0.1%," Schneider said. "We also expect IBM to guide for over $15.0 bn in free cash flow (we model $15.1 bn vs. the Street at $15.0 bn). We think investors will remain focused on Software performance given the recent Confluent acquisition announcement and expectations of ongoing double-digit growth in Red Hat throughout 2026."

Last month, IBM revealed its plan to acquire Confluent (CFLT), a pioneer in data streaming, for about $11B. The deal is expected to close by the middle of 2026. The acquisition must still be approved by Confluent shareholders, regulatory agencies and other standard closing conditions.

Goldman Sachs also expects IBM to focus on a recovery in its Consulting segment, particularly as the mainframe cycle begins to normalize. 

For IBM's Q4 2026 results, a consensus estimate expects the company to report adjusted EPS of $4.30, GAAP EPS of $3.71 and revenue totaling $19.23B.

Last week, Jefferies upgraded IBM to Buy from Neutral due to expected software growth in 2026.

data.title: Semiconductor sales weaken in November, but NAND still a bright spot: MS
data.description:
Semiconductor sales softened a bit in November, though the memory market continued to shine, Morgan Stanley said, citing data from the industry's top trade group.

According to the Semiconductor Industry Association, sales during November rose 7.1% month-over-month, below Morgan Stanley's estimate of 10.4% growth, but above the 10-year average of 2.5% growth. Three month growth accelerated to 29.8% year-over-year, compared to 27.2%, the investment firm said. One month growth soared 29.5% year-over-year, Morgan Stanley added.

Breaking it down by region, Asia Pacific saw sales soar 71.9%, while China also saw a healthy gain of 28.9%. The Americas and Europe followed, as the regions saw 12.4% and 10.8% growth, respectively. Only Japan saw a decline, as the region saw a 5.6% contraction.

“We expect m/m bumpiness to continue to some degree, though the broader recovery remains intact and numbers come up due to memory pricing,” Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Joseph Moore, wrote in a note to clients. “SIA data broadly underperformed seasonality, with NAND a bright spot relative to other markets.” 

Breaking it down by sector, discretes missed estimates, as the sector contracted 4.1% month-over-month, below Morgan Stanley's estimate of flat growth and below the 0.7% decline over the 10-year average. Units were above the 10-year average, at a decline of 0.5%, compared to a 1.8% decline, while the average selling price fell 4.6%, below the historical average of a 1.2% gain.

The analog portion of the market was also weak in November, as sales fell 4.4%, below the 3% decline Morgan Stanley was expecting and the 10-year historical average of a 3.2% decline. Units were below the 10-year average (down2.4%, compared to a 2% decline), while the average selling price fell 2.1%, below the 10-year average of a 1.2% decline.

Microcontrollers also missed estimates, down 7.3% month-over-month, compared to the analyst estimates of flat and the 0.6% decline over the 10-year historical average. Units fell 9.2%, compared to the 2.9% decline over the 10-year average, while the average selling price fell 2%, below the 10-year average of a 2.8% decline.

Microprocessor units were in-line with estimates, at down 0.3%, compared to the analyst estimate of flat and the 10-year historical average of flat.

Memory was mixed, as NAND outperformed seasonality, while dynamic random access memory moderated from what was seen as an “exceptional” October. NAND was above expectations and the 5-year average, at 47.3% month-over-month growth, compared to the analyst estimate of 40.8% and the 5-year historical average of 21.9% growth. Bits rose 37.8% month-over-month, while the average selling price growth was 6.9%, below the analyst estimate of 13.4% growth. 

“While data center SSD markets are meaningfully constrained, we have seen some demand pulled forward in consumer markets anticipating supply tightness to come as supply moves to enterprise,” the analysts explained.

DRAM saw 18.9% month-over-month growth, below the 43.4% analyst estimate and 5-year historical average of 26.3% growth. Bits rose 18.2%, below the 36% analyst estimate, while the 0.6% uptick in the average selling price was below the 5.4% forecast.

“The context here is severe supply constraints forcing a more linear pattern — and prices moved materially higher in December,” the analysts explained.

Despite the slight dip in November sales, Morgan Stanley's analysts are still bullish on the semiconductor industry and said it favors certain parts and companies, including NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) and Analog Devices (ADI).

“While we remain mindful that short-term data are likely to remain uneven, we have become incrementally more constructive as supporting data points accumulate, favoring companies with a combination of structural positioning and improving cyclical exposure, including NXPI and ADI,” the analysts wrote.

As for the AI trade, “direct beneficiaries” such as Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Astera Labs (ALAB), Micron (MU), Sandisk (SNDK), Applied Materials (AMAT) and MKS Inc. (MKSI) should continue to see “robust demand.”

“With respect to AI, we continue to hold the view — unchanged from October — that strength in AI and improvements in the real economy are not mutually exclusive. AI demand is increasingly proliferating into analog, most notably through power applications, though we continue to see the more material step-up tied to 800V architecture deployment which is further out,” the analysts explained. “In the meantime, direct AI beneficiaries (NVDA, AVGO, ALAB, MU, SNDK, and SPE players AMAT and MKS) remain supported by robust demand dynamics that show little sign of slowing — even 'skyrocketing' per Nvidia at CES last week — reinforcing our view that exposure across both AI-linked and broad-based semiconductor recovery themes remains warranted.”

data.title: Alcoa, Kaiser Aluminum cut at Wells Fargo; Constellium new favorite aluminum processor
data.description:
Alcoa (AA) and Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) turned slightly lower in choppy trading Tuesday as Wells Fargo downgraded both stocks to Equal Weight from Overweight, believing investors may be underestimating substitution risks for aluminum and that the recent surge in the stock prices are not sustainable.

Alcoa's (AA) valuation seems to fully reflect the recent strong run in aluminum prices, Wells Fargo analyst Timna Tanners said in her downgrade, even while raising her stock price target to $71 from $58, using 5x estimated 2026 EBITDA marked-to-market at recent spot LME aluminum prices at ~$3,200/ton, but the company has less exposure to aluminum given that its alumina segment comprised nearly half of estimated 2025 EBITDA and alumina prices have been depressed.

The analyst said she does not see imminent relief from alumina market weakness, and has little conviction of tariff relief for Alcoa's (AA) operations in Canada, with no apparent benefit for the Trump administration.

Tanners also downgraded Kaiser Aluminum (KALU), saying valuation looks full and now reflects margin expansion from its roll coat ramp up into 2026, while raising her stock PT to $120
from $108, reflecting an unchanged 8x estimated 2026 EV/EBITDA, consistent with its historical average.

The analyst upgraded Constellium (CSTM) to Overweight from Equal Weight, seeing ~25% of its business helped by the blowout in the U.S. aluminum Midwest premium spread vs. used beverage can costs, and raised its price target to $25 from $17, reflecting an unchanged 6x estimated 2026 EBITDA but now assuming EBITDA up 21% from estimated 2025 levels at $704M.

She now prefers Constellium (CSTM) to Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) as her favorite aluminum processor, saying Constellium benefits more from expanding scrap spreads.

data.title: BioAge Labs extends rally after updating early-stage trial data for lead drug
data.description:
Shares of BioAge Labs (BIOA) continued to spike for the second consecutive session on Tuesday in reaction to updated results from an early-stage trial for the company’s lead asset, BGE-102, targeted at cardiovascular risk factors.

Citing interim data from an ongoing Phase 1 clinical trial, the Richmond, California-based biotech said on Monday that by Day 14, the oral therapy had caused an 86% median reduction in high-sensitivity C-reactive protein ((hsCRP)), a biomarker of inflammation.

Notably, the company added that 93% of patients on BGE-102 saw their hsCRP levels fall below 2 mg/L—the level associated with lower cardiovascular risk. The readout represented the trial's multiple ascending dose cohort, which included obese patients with elevated inflammation.

“Chronic inflammation is now recognized as a major driver of cardiovascular disease—on par with cholesterol—yet it remains far less commonly treated,” noted CEO Kristen Fortney.

The experimental therapy was safe and well-tolerated in the study, BioAge (BIOA) said, adding that adverse events were infrequent and mild to moderate in severity, a tolerability profile observed in a December readout from the same trial.

The company expects to report full Phase 1 results and begin a Phase 2a trial for BGE-102 in H2 2026.

data.title: U.S. stocks slip as retail inflation comes in slightly cooler, earnings fail to lift sentiment
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Tuesday, with the Nasdaq Composite ((COMP:IND)) showing some strength, as investors assessed cooler-than-expected core CPI inflation and some important earnings reports.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.4% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.7%.

The December Consumer Price Index came in at +0.3% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.7% YoY vs. +2.6% consensus and +2.7% prior.

Core CPI (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.6% YoY vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior.

“Tuesday's CPI was in-line with expectations and that will likely keep the Federal Reserve on pause when it comes to interest rates for the foreseeable future… Recent positive employment data, elevated inflation, sticky price levels and political noise will keep the Fed at bay through at least the Spring,” said Skyler Weinand, CIO at Regan Capital.

“CPI is still under a cloud from the missing October number. Inflation is still relatively high, but trending downward. Consumers are still grappling with high overall price levels and sticker shock from the post-Covid jarring in housing, healthcare, education and everyday purchase prices,” Weinand added.

Also, new home sales slipped 0.1% MoM to 737K in October, vs. 716K expected, from 738K in the prior month, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Among companies reporting on Tuesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) -3.9% posted better-than-expected earnings, while Delta Air Lines (DAL) -2.4% had year-over-year revenue growth. 

Big companies reporting this week include Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Citigroup (C).

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower to 4.17%, while the U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was also 1 basis point lower to 3.53%. A 30-Year bond auction traded through.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase beat largely due to lower expenses, analysts say
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) stock slid 4.0% in Tuesday afternoon trading as the bank's Q4 earnings beat consensus, largely on lower-than-expected operating expenses, according to equity research notes. Meanwhile, 2026 guidance was in line with analysts' expectations.

Investment banking fees, which fell 5% Y/Y, were disappointing.

The bank also remains under pressure as the Trump administration moves to limit credit card interest and swipe fees, which are strong revenue generators for many banks. During JPMorgan Chase's (JPM) earnings call, Chief Financial Officer Jeremy Barnum said "everything is on the table" in its efforts to push back against the proposed 10% interest rate on credit cards.

In analyzing the company's Q4 earnings, Wolfe Research analyst Steven Chubak pointed out that, "Expenses were well controlled, as efficiency of 51.3% came in below WR est./Consensus of 52.2%/52.9%." Also, net interest income ((NII)) of $25.0B slightly topped consensus of $24.9B and Wolfe's $24.8B estimate, resulting from better loan growth and slightly better net interest margin expansion.

Evercore ISI analyst Glenn Schorr points out that "NII continues to be constrained by lower rates, and home lending was still down 4% Y/Y."

Citi analyst Keith Horowitz credits the ~$0.20 beat on preprovision net revenue to consensus on the improved expense management and the slight beat in NII, ex-markets.

Adjusted for one-time items, "core expense beat was in large part on compensation within CIB (Corporate and Investment Banking)," Horowitz wrote.

Equities trading fees also pitched in to the beat, at $2.9B, beating consensus of $2.7B and Wolfe's estimate of $2.8B, Chubak noted.

One factor to note, JPMorgan's (JPM) provision for credit losses of $4.66B, includes a $2.2B reserve taken for its upcoming purchase of the Apple credit portfolio. That reduced its GAAP net EPS by $0.60. Excluding the item, Q4 adjusted EPS was $5.23.

On the negative side of the Q4 ledger, Investment Banking fees were down 5% Y/Y, "slightly worse than conference guidance of up low-single digit; ECM (equity capital markets) was down -16% Y/Y (-21% Q/Q), Advisory was down -3% Y/Y (+12% Q/Q), while DCM (debt capital markets) was down -2% Y/Y (-24% Q/Q)," Evercore ISI's Schorr wrote in a note to clients. 

In looking ahead, analysts call the company's 2026 guidance largely in line. "We see no real surprises to 2026 guidance with $95B NII, excluding markets, consistent with previous guidance given at 3Q25 earnings, $105B ’26 expense guide maintained, and 3.4% card NCO in-line with implied December guidance range of 3.3-3.6%," Citi's Horowitz said.

data.title: SA Quant ratings: Best and worst-rated consumer staples stocks ahead of Q4 earnings
data.description:
With the fourth quarter earnings season approaching, Seeking Alpha generated a list of the highest- and lowest-rated mid- to mega-cap consumer staples stocks as per its Quant model.

Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) was the third-worst performing sector in Q4, seeing a marginal loss of 0.73%, compared to the broader market SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) (SP500) gain of 2%. 

Staples saw a defensive lag and are said to be less susceptible to economic cycles, frequently lagging in a risk-on market. The XLP Fund (XLP) holds a ~5.8% weightage in the S&P 500 index (SP500). Among the 11 sector-based ETFs, Seeking Alpha's quant ratings give the S&P consumer staples fund (XLP) the second-lowest score of 2.28, only above the real estate ETF (XLRE). 

Quant ratings

Turning to individual stocks, Seeking Alpha's quant rating system gives the following companies top ratings:

  • Ambev (ABEV) - Strong Buy with a quant rating of 4.86
  • United Natural Foods (UNFI) - Strong Buy rating of 4.73
  • Danone (DANOY) - Buy rating of 4.38
  • Jerónimo Martins (JRONY) - Buy rating 4.26
  • Heineken (HEINY) - Buy rating 4.18

Among the lowest SA quant-rated companies, all having a Strong Sell ratings are:

  • BellRing Brands (BRBR) - Strong sell with quant rating of 1.10
  • e.l.f. Beauty (ELF) - 1.22
  • Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) - 1.25
  • Primo Brands (PRMB) - 1.28
  • National Beverage (FIZZ) - 1.35

Analyst comments

Forecasting 2026, "Favor attractive & profitable categories amid a challenged Staples backdrop - Buy nicotine, energy drink, candy and beauty stocks" Goldman Sachs Research wrote in a recent note. The research house cited top ideas for 2026, including

  • Market share gainers and strong topline performers: (PM) and (MNST)
  • Where bearishness appears over-done: (PEP), (ELF), (CELH), (HSY) and (SFM)
  • Beer recovery: (STZ) and (TAP)
  • Growth-advantaged emerging market exposure: (PM), (MDLZ) and (CL)
  • Easing cost pressure: (HSY)
  • GLP-1/better-for-you positioning: (SFM)

"Although the consumer staples sector shares some similarities and is even more resilient when it comes to macroeconomic impacts, I am more bearish on this industry in the short term, and I believe that the current situation is much more complex than it appears to be." said SA contributor Kenio Fontes.

Other funds for a diversified approach to consumer staples include: Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC); iShares U.S. Consumer Staples ETF (IYK); First Trust Consumer Staples AlphaDEX Fund (FXG); Invesco Dynamic Food & Beverage ETF (PBJ); First Trust Nasdaq Food & Beverage ETF (FTXG); iShares Global Consumer Staples ETF (KXI).

data.title: Travere plummets as FDA extends Filspari review for rare kidney disorder
data.description:
• The US FDA has extended by three months to April 13 its review of an sNDA from Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) to expand the label for Filspari (sparsentan) for focal segmental glomerulosclerosis ("FSGS"), a rare kidney disorder.
  • Following a trading halt that began at 1240p ET, shares are now down ~20%. 
  • The company said that recent information provided to the agency on the drug's clinical benefit constituted a Major Amendment to the application, necessitating the additional time.
  • Travere added no additional information on safety or manufacturing is being requested.
